card.intuition: A bit array of m bits, all initialised to 0. To insert, set k hash-derived bit positions to 1. To query, check all k positions — if any is 0, the element is definitely absent; if all are 1, it's probably present (may be a false positive). False negatives are impossible.
card.analogy: A porous net that catches fish: every real fish gets caught (no false negatives). But occasionally a clump of seaweed (non-fish) also happens to pass through the net's pattern and triggers all the same catches — a false positive.
card.time_complexity: O(k) per insert and query (k = number of hash functions)
card.space_complexity: O(m) bits — much smaller than a hash set
when_to_use: Cache filtering (Google BigTable uses Bloom filters to avoid unnecessary disk lookups)
Web crawlers: quick check if a URL has been visited
Spell checkers, network routers — any case where false positives are tolerable but space is tight
gotchas: Bloom filters do NOT support deletion (bit clearing would create false negatives) — use counting Bloom filter for deletions
False positive rate ≈ (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k — minimised at k = (m/n) ln 2
Choosing m bits: for p% false positive rate with n items, m ≈ -n ln(p) / (ln 2)²

exercise.prompt: Implement a Bloom filter using k hash functions (simulated via salted hashing).
hints: add: set self.bits[pos] = 1 for each position from _positions(item)
might_contain: check all positions returned by _positions(item) — return True only if ALL are 1
exercise.test_code:
bf = BloomFilter(m=500, k=3)
for word in ['apple','banana','cherry','date','elderberry']:
    bf.add(word)
# No false negatives
for word in ['apple','banana','cherry','date','elderberry']:
    assert bf.might_contain(word), f'{word} should be present'
# Most non-members should return False
non_members = ['fig','grape','honeydew','kiwi','lemon']
fp = sum(bf.might_contain(w) for w in non_members)
assert fp <= 2, f'Too many false positives: {fp}/{len(non_members)}'
print('PASS')
